title: Typed ID and Opaque Identifier Patterns for Inference APIs
preview:
Overview:
This document specifies recommended patterns for representing identifiers (IDs) in Redwood Inference services and SDKs. The goal is to increase type safety, reduce accidental cross-tenant or cross-resource ID mixing, make telemetry more explicit, and enable safer migrations between identifier formats. Thes...
text:
Overview:
This document specifies recommended patterns for representing identifiers (IDs) in Redwood Inference services and SDKs. The goal is to increase type safety, reduce accidental cross-tenant or cross-resource ID mixing, make telemetry more explicit, and enable safer migrations between identifier formats. These recommendations apply to public-facing inference APIs, internal service-to-service RPCs, and developer SDKs.

Why this matters:
- Simple untyped strings lead to accidental misuse (e.g., passing a model_id where a deployment_id is required).
- Observable signals and logs lose structure when different ID classes are conflated.
- Security and auditability improve when IDs are clearly typed and opaque where appropriate.
- Backwards-incompatible ID shape changes (e.g., adding versioning prefixes) are safer when wrapped by a typed adapter.

Scope:
- Applies to all new APIs and ...

text: Defines the Execution Quiet Mode (EQM) concept and an inter-shard preemption consensus policy to reduce tail-latency regressions from aggressive preemption in mixed-priority multi-tenant inference workloads. Specifies contract-level API hints, runtime config surface, telemetry requirements, rollout and rollback steps, and safety gates.

title: API Telemetry and Cost-SLO Governance Playbook
preview:
Summary:
This playbook defines a company-wide standard for tying API-level telemetry to cost signals and SLO governance. It prescribes the required telemetry envelope, SLI definitions that include cost attributes (token-cost, GPU-seconds, egress bytes), sampling and retention rules, alert gates, and rollout steps fo...
text:
Summary:
This playbook defines a company-wide standard for tying API-level telemetry to cost signals and SLO governance. It prescribes the required telemetry envelope, SLI definitions that include cost attributes (token-cost, GPU-seconds, egress bytes), sampling and retention rules, alert gates, and rollout steps for adopting cost-aware SLOs across Redwood services.

Motivation:
- Inference economics are a first-class operational concern at Redwood. Teams must be able to reason about unit cost alongside latency/availability.
- Current SLOs are primarily latency/availability focused; missing cost signals lead to reactive and inconsistent capacity decisions.
- A standardized telemetry + cost tagging model enables automated budget enforcement, chargeback, and smarter runtime routing (e.g., cheaper model variant fallback when cost SLO is breached).

Scope and Applicability:
- Applies to a...

title: ADR: request watermarking and SLO-preserving shedding
preview:
Summary
-------
This ADR establishes 'request watermarking' as the runtime protocol and a coordinated shedding policy to preserve tail latency SLOs under high-load or degraded GPU capacity. Watermarking attaches lightweight budget and fidelity metadata to each inference request at the API edge, enabling downstream r...
text:
Summary
-------
This ADR establishes 'request watermarking' as the runtime protocol and a coordinated shedding policy to preserve tail latency SLOs under high-load or degraded GPU capacity. Watermarking attaches lightweight budget and fidelity metadata to each inference request at the API edge, enabling downstream runtime actors (scheduler, KV cache, and model router) to make deterministic, SLO-aware shedding and fidelity decisions.

Motivation
----------
Redwood serves heterogeneous workloads with strict 99th/99.9th percentile latency SLOs on shared GPU pools. When capacity is constrained (bursting, partial GPU failures, or noisy neighbors) indiscriminate admission control or FIFO shedding causes disproportionate user impact and cascading quality regressions. We need a deterministic, observable, and composable mechanism that: 
- Encodes client intent and budget on each request so run...

title: Geo Routing Control Plane Architecture (2026)
preview:
## Overview
This page describes Redwood’s standard architecture for multi-region traffic steering across the edge and control plane. It covers the routing primitives (GeoDNS and Anycast), the control-plane model for policy compilation and distribution, and the operational behavior for failover and rollouts.

This do...
text:
## Overview
This page describes Redwood’s standard architecture for multi-region traffic steering across the edge and control plane. It covers the routing primitives (GeoDNS and Anycast), the control-plane model for policy compilation and distribution, and the operational behavior for failover and rollouts.

This document replaces earlier design notes in Drive and should be treated as the current reference for implementation and operations.

## Scope
This standard applies to:
- Ingress routing for customer-facing inference endpoints (HTTP API) and websocket streaming endpoints.
- Control-plane APIs and distribution for routing policies, regional weights, and real-time routing overrides.
- Failure and fallback behavior, plus required observability to support platform SLOs.

## Non-goals
- Vendor-specific router/BGP configuration details.
- Deep dive into egress/data-plane security (TLS...

title: Sandboxed-extension-and-observability-adaptor-contract
preview:
Summary:
This page defines the contract and safe-implementation playbook for third-party or internal runtime extensions that attach to Redwood inference services via a lightweight adaptor (sidecar or plugin). The goal is to allow extensibility (custom routing, enrichers, feature flags, policy checks, and observabili...
text:
Summary:
This page defines the contract and safe-implementation playbook for third-party or internal runtime extensions that attach to Redwood inference services via a lightweight adaptor (sidecar or plugin). The goal is to allow extensibility (custom routing, enrichers, feature flags, policy checks, and observability hooks) while preserving SLOs, tenant isolation, resource safety, and privacy requirements. This is a cross-cutting standard: it applies to API gateways, runtime sidecars, private deployments, and Hosted API integrations.

Why we need it:
- Demand for custom behavior (enterprise filtering, telemetry enrichment, domain-specific prompt transforms) is increasing.
- Unconstrained extensions risk latency SLOs, telemetry noise, PII leakage, and resource exhaustion.
- We need a repeatable, auditable path that enables safe extension with clear operational controls.

Scope and app...

title: Capability Signal Profiles and Client Negotiation Playbook
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook defines a compact, observable protocol for advertising runtime capabilities and negotiating effective behavior between clients and Redwood inference endpoints. The protocol reduces brittle client checks, enables safe feature rollouts, and provides structured signals for debugging and...
text:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook defines a compact, observable protocol for advertising runtime capabilities and negotiating effective behavior between clients and Redwood inference endpoints. The protocol reduces brittle client checks, enables safe feature rollouts, and provides structured signals for debugging and cost accounting.

Scope: Applies to HTTP and gRPC endpoints that expose evolving inference features (streaming modes, structured outputs, function/tool calling semantics, fidelity/quantization hints, early-exit heuristics). Exempt: purely documentation-only changes and telemetry-only fields.

Background and motivation
- Frequent client breakages occurred when runtime features changed without an explicit compatibility surface.
- Feature guards and ad-hoc query params proved inadequate for multi-variant deployments and private/legacy tenants.
- A negotiation layer allows cli...

title: Hardware Telemetry: Annotated Billing & Usage Schema
preview:
Overview

This document defines the canonical telemetry envelope, field-level annotations, and aggregation rules used for hardware-level billing and usage accounting across Redwood Inference GPU and accelerator fleets. It describes the required schema for per-request and per-batch telemetry, encryption and redaction...
text:
Overview

This document defines the canonical telemetry envelope, field-level annotations, and aggregation rules used for hardware-level billing and usage accounting across Redwood Inference GPU and accelerator fleets. It describes the required schema for per-request and per-batch telemetry, encryption and redaction rules, sampling/aggregation guidance, SLOs for ingestion and billing pipelines, and a migration plan for rolling the schema to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.

Goals
- Produce a single, auditable canonical record that maps runtime signals to billable units (GPU-seconds, memory-hours, energy-kWh).
- Avoid leakage of customer PII or sensitive prompt content into billing datasets.
- Ensure forward/backwards compatibility for model and hardware changes.
- Provide clear owner and rollout steps to minimize chargeback disruption.

Scope
Applies to: runtime serving-sid...

title: Shadow Traffic Synthesis and Acceptance Playbook
preview:
Summary
This playbook defines the standard process, tooling, and quantitative acceptance criteria for using shadow traffic (synthetic or mirrored) to validate model variants, serving runtime changes, and routing policies before progressive rollouts. Shadow traffic testing is required for all significant inference-si...
text:
Summary
This playbook defines the standard process, tooling, and quantitative acceptance criteria for using shadow traffic (synthetic or mirrored) to validate model variants, serving runtime changes, and routing policies before progressive rollouts. Shadow traffic testing is required for all significant inference-side changes that could affect latency, correctness, or cost (new model variants, quantization changes, runtime kernel upgrades, routing changes, and batching policy adjustments).

Scope and intent
- Scope: applies to hosted Redwood API, Dedicated pools, and Private runtime variants.
- Intent: catch regressions in latency, error behavior, output quality, resource utilization, and telemetry before exposing requests to production traffic.
- Not a replacement for production canaries; designed to be a high-fidelity, low-risk verification step.

Roles and ownership
1. Change owner...

title: adr-traffic-tagging-cost-allocation-2026
preview:
Summary:

This ADR documents the decision to adopt a fine-grained traffic tagging and telemetry enrichment model to support per-tenant cost allocation, cost-aware routing, and improved observability. The goal is to enable accurate billing attribution for multi-tenant workloads, provide signal for routing/capacity de...
text:
Summary:

This ADR documents the decision to adopt a fine-grained traffic tagging and telemetry enrichment model to support per-tenant cost allocation, cost-aware routing, and improved observability. The goal is to enable accurate billing attribution for multi-tenant workloads, provide signal for routing/capacity decisions, and preserve tenant privacy and performance.

Context and problem statement:

- Redwood runs mixed tenancy across Hosted API, Dedicated pools, and Private deployments. The cost of inference (GPU time, host CPU, memory, network egress) must be attributed to customers for billing and internal chargeback.
- Existing telemetry streams capture coarse route and model metrics but lack deterministic per-request cost attribution (e.g., token counts attributed to tenant + route + model variant).
- We need a solution that:
 - Correlates inference resource consumption to a ten...

text:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood’s **minimum standards** for reporting and acting on FP8/INT8 kernel benchmark results. It exists to ensure:
- Results are **reproducible**, **comparable**, and **decision-grade**.
- We can safely translate measured wins into **kernel auto-selection** and **scheduler defaults**.
- Any internal or external performance claims are **defensible** and properly **footnoted**.

These standards apply to:
- Kernel microbenchmarks (e.g., attention / matmul shape sweeps)
- End-to-end serving benchmarks (prefill/decode mixes, batching, concurrency)
- Perf canary and CI perf gates for quantized-kernel rollouts

---

## Scope and non-goals
**In scope**
- FP8/INT8 kernels and related runtime settings (dispatch, alignment constraints, KV cache behavior)
- Scheduler interactions that change realized wins (continuous batching, prefill/decode interleaving)
- Hosted ru...

title: ADR-019: FP8/INT8 kernel auto-selection heuristics (Hosted Runtime)
preview:
## Status
**Accepted** (implemented behind flags; default-on gated by perf canary and eval sign-off).

## Summary
This ADR defines the runtime’s **kernel auto-selection heuristics** for choosing **FP8**, **INT8**, or **baseline (BF16/FP16)** kernel paths for attention and GEMMs during LLM inference. The heuristics a...
text:
## Status
**Accepted** (implemented behind flags; default-on gated by perf canary and eval sign-off).

## Summary
This ADR defines the runtime’s **kernel auto-selection heuristics** for choosing **FP8**, **INT8**, or **baseline (BF16/FP16)** kernel paths for attention and GEMMs during LLM inference. The heuristics are driven by:
- **GPU architecture** (H100/Hopper, A100/Ampere, L4/Ada where applicable)
- **Model shape** (hidden size, head dim, KV head dim, alignment constraints)
- **Sequence-length regime** (prefill vs decode; short vs long context)
- **Workload characteristics** (batch size, concurrency, and prefill/decode mix)

The decision includes:
- A prioritized selection order per GPU + workload regime
- Explicit **shape guardrails** (alignment, min/max dimensions)
- **Fallback rules** (correctness, unsupported shapes, quality gating)
- Rollout controls (flags, canary cohorts, ...

title: Audit Log Events – Emission & Logging Standard (Schema v1)
preview:
## Purpose
This document defines the engineering standard for *emitting* audit log events across Redwood Inference services (Hosted, Dedicated, and Private). The goal is to ensure events are:
- Consistent (same action → same `event_type` and field semantics)
- Investigable (actor/target/correlation present)

text:
## Purpose
This document defines the engineering standard for *emitting* audit log events across Redwood Inference services (Hosted, Dedicated, and Private). The goal is to ensure events are:
- Consistent (same action → same `event_type` and field semantics)
- Investigable (actor/target/correlation present)
- Safe (PII/secrets minimized + redaction enforced)
- SIEM-friendly (stable top-level fields, controlled cardinality)
- Operable (clear delivery semantics and behavior during outages)

This is an emission standard (producer responsibilities). For full schema definition, see: **Audit Log Schema v1**.

---

## Scope
Applies to all services and tools that perform or mediate sensitive actions, including:
- API Gateway (admin endpoints, authn/authz control surfaces)
- Control Plane services (tenant/project config, RBAC, API keys/credentials, policy)
- Console backend (admin workflows, e...

title: ADR: Suggestions API contract, stable suggestion IDs, and pagination (Console Suggestions beta)
preview:
## Status
Published (effective for Console Suggestions beta).

## Context
Redwood Console is shipping a beta “Optimization suggestions” experience focused on batching and caching recommendations. The Console needs a stable, predictable API contract so it can:
- Render a list view with sorting/filtering and a details...
text:
## Status
Published (effective for Console Suggestions beta).

## Context
Redwood Console is shipping a beta “Optimization suggestions” experience focused on batching and caching recommendations. The Console needs a stable, predictable API contract so it can:
- Render a list view with sorting/filtering and a details drawer.
- Track user actions (impression/expand/dismiss/apply) against a stable identifier.
- Support deep links and “dismissed” state that survives recomputation.
- Handle pagination safely for large orgs/projects.

The Optimize Suggestions service recomputes suggestions on a cadence (hourly/daily) based on telemetry aggregates. This creates inherent churn: suggestions may be added/removed and estimates may change even when the underlying opportunity is “the same.” Without stable IDs and a clear pagination strategy, the Console will show flicker, lose dismissals, and risk...

text:
## Status
Accepted (v0) for Console Suggestions Beta. This ADR defines the risk score inputs, thresholds, confidence handling, and UI mapping for batching/caching suggestions.

## Decision owners
- Primary: Sana Farid (Risk/confidence data science)
- Approvers: Dr. Maya Srinivasan (Applied ML sponsor), Sean Gallagher (SRE rollout lead)
- Stakeholders: Aditya Rao (Optimize PM), Marissa Cole (Console PM), Dylan Brooks (Console Eng)

## Context
The Console “Optimization suggestions” beta surfaces batching and caching recommendations with:
1) estimated cost savings ($/day and %)
2) estimated performance impact (p50/p95 latency deltas)
3) a risk level (Low/Medium/High) with a clear explanation and guardrails

We need a conservative, explainable, and stable risk scoring approach that:
- is easy to reason about by customers and internal teams
- avoids overconfident labels when telemetry is s...

title: Quality Metrics Observability Standard (Shadow Traffic + Rollouts)
preview:

- Hosted API
- Dedicated
- Private (VPC / on-prem)

This document covers:
- Metric naming and tagging (dimension) conven...
text:
## Purpose
This standard defines how Redwood emits, aggregates, and presents *quality* metrics produced by shadow traffic and rollout experiments. It is intended to make metrics comparable across:
- Hosted API
- Dedicated
- Private (VPC / on-prem)

This document covers:
- Metric naming and tagging (dimension) conventions
- Sampling math and how to compute unbiased aggregates
- Aggregation windows and minimum sample requirements
- Confidence interval (CI) and delta reporting requirements
- Dashboard conventions and recommended views

Out of scope:
- The scoring rubric definitions themselves (see rubrics doc)
- Data retention/redaction policy details (see security doc)

---

## Definitions
- **Control**: The production response returned to the end-user.
- **Shadow target / Candidate**: The mirrored variant that does *not* affect the user response (e.g., new model version, quant profile,...

title: ADR-0143: Error Budget Burn-Rate Alerting Approach (Multi-window)
preview:
## Summary
We will use **multi-window error-budget burn-rate alerting** (Google SRE-style) as the canonical alerting approach for tiered SLOs (Hosted vs Dedicated). Alerts will be computed from the same SLIs that drive SLO reporting and error budget accounting.

This ADR defines:
- How we compute burn rate (relative...
text:
## Summary
We will use **multi-window error-budget burn-rate alerting** (Google SRE-style) as the canonical alerting approach for tiered SLOs (Hosted vs Dedicated). Alerts will be computed from the same SLIs that drive SLO reporting and error budget accounting.

This ADR defines:
- How we compute burn rate (relative to a tier’s SLO)
- Standard alert window pairs and thresholds for paging vs ticketing
- Guardrails (minimum traffic, missing data behavior)
- Trade-offs vs static threshold alerting (e.g., “5xx > 1%”)

## Status
**Accepted** and implemented as the default for tiered SLO alerting starting Q1 2026 rollout (warn-only enforcement phase).

## Context / Problem
Redwood Inference is formalizing tiered SLOs and an enforced error budget policy. We need an alerting strategy that:
- **Aligns directly** with customer-facing reliability commitments (SLOs) across tiers
- Detects both **...

title: ADR-021: Standardize Cost Model Interfaces (Canonical Spec + Cost Model API)
preview:
## Status
Accepted (Published)

## Context
Redwood Inference currently has multiple, partially overlapping ways to represent and compute inference cost:
- Console prototypes compute cost breakdowns from telemetry aggregation jobs.
- Optimize prototypes compute “what-if” ROI using separate assumptions and field names...
text:
## Status
Accepted (Published)

## Context
Redwood Inference currently has multiple, partially overlapping ways to represent and compute inference cost:
- Console prototypes compute cost breakdowns from telemetry aggregation jobs.
- Optimize prototypes compute “what-if” ROI using separate assumptions and field names.
- Billing/quota hooks use yet another set of primitives and accounting splits.

This has led to:
- Inconsistent customer-facing numbers (Console vs invoice) and inconsistent internal reporting.
- Unclear semantics for “what counts” (prefill vs decode, cache hits, batch efficiency, quantization impact, and overhead).
- Duplicated engineering effort and fragile integrations when any one surface evolves.
- Operational risk: drift investigations are slowed by lack of a single “source of truth” representation.

We need to unify inputs/outputs so Console (dashboards/observabili...

title: ADR: Audit Log Schema v1 and Sensitive-Action Event Taxonomy
preview:
## Status
Published (Schema v1)

## Context
Redwood Inference is standardizing audit logging across Hosted, Dedicated, and especially Private deployments. Customers and internal Security/GRC require consistent audit logs to:
- Investigate suspicious behavior and support incident response.
- Satisfy SOC 2 / ISO-align...
text:
## Status
Published (Schema v1)

## Context
Redwood Inference is standardizing audit logging across Hosted, Dedicated, and especially Private deployments. Customers and internal Security/GRC require consistent audit logs to:
- Investigate suspicious behavior and support incident response.
- Satisfy SOC 2 / ISO-aligned control expectations (who did what, when, and where).
- Export events to SIEMs (Splunk, Azure Sentinel/Log Analytics, Datadog) with predictable semantics.

Historically, audit events have been emitted inconsistently across services and deployment modes:
- Divergent field names (e.g., user_id vs actor_id, org vs tenant).
- Inconsistent nesting and structure, complicating SIEM parsing.
- High-cardinality / free-form strings leading to unusable dashboards and increased SIEM cost.
- Gaps in coverage for “sensitive actions” (RBAC, credential lifecycle, policy/routing changes,...

title: Rollouts + SLO auto-mitigation metrics: naming and labeling standard
preview:

This page defines the canonical metric names and label sets for:
- Rollout steps (canary/A-B/staged deployments)
- SLO evaluation (latency/error-rate/saturation signals)
- Automated actions (pause/rollback/shift traffic)
- Suppression and “no-action” reasons (debounce/holdoff, missing monitors, admin over...
text:
## Purpose
This page defines the canonical metric names and label sets for:
- Rollout steps (canary/A-B/staged deployments)
- SLO evaluation (latency/error-rate/saturation signals)
- Automated actions (pause/rollback/shift traffic)
- Suppression and “no-action” reasons (debounce/holdoff, missing monitors, admin overrides)

The goals are:
1) Consistent dashboards and alert rules across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.
2) Reliable cardinality characteristics (safe for Prometheus/Grafana + long-retention backends).
3) Clear linkability from metrics → audit events → rollout UI.

This standard is used by:
- rollout-controller
- policy-engine
- slo-toolkit / SLO monitor provider
- incident-bot notifications

## Non-goals
- This page does not define *SLO thresholds* or *policy defaults* (see: SRE SLO definitions for rollout safety).
- This page does not define audit event schemas ...

title: SDK Retry & Timeout Standard (v1)
preview:
## Purpose
This document defines Redwood Inference’s company-wide standard for **SDK retry/backoff** and **timeout behavior** across Python, TypeScript, and Go SDKs.

The goals are:
- Predictable behavior across languages (“same input, same failure semantics”).
- Avoid retry storms while still handling transient fai...
text:
## Purpose
This document defines Redwood Inference’s company-wide standard for **SDK retry/backoff** and **timeout behavior** across Python, TypeScript, and Go SDKs.

The goals are:
- Predictable behavior across languages (“same input, same failure semantics”).
- Avoid retry storms while still handling transient failures safely.
- Respect platform signals (e.g., **Retry-After**, request IDs) and preserve debuggability.
- Provide consistent configuration knobs and typed errors.

Non-goals:
- Redefining Redwood API error shapes beyond what the gateway already emits.
- Adding new auth mechanisms.

## Definitions
- **Attempt**: a single HTTP request execution.
- **Retry**: re-issuing an attempt after a failure and delay.
- **Retry budget**: the maximum number of attempts and/or maximum total elapsed time spent retrying.
- **Overall timeout**: the maximum time allowed for the full request ...

title: ADR-022: Standard Cost Breakdown Taxonomy (v1)
preview:
## Status
Published (v1). Any changes require an ADR addendum or ADR-022 revision.

## Context
Redwood Console and Redwood Optimize both present “cost” to users, while telemetry pipelines and billing hooks compute similar-but-not-identical numbers. This has caused:
- Conflicting prefill vs decode splits (runtime vs ...
text:
## Status
Published (v1). Any changes require an ADR addendum or ADR-022 revision.

## Context
Redwood Console and Redwood Optimize both present “cost” to users, while telemetry pipelines and billing hooks compute similar-but-not-identical numbers. This has caused:
- Conflicting prefill vs decode splits (runtime vs analytics derived).
- Ambiguous treatment of KV/prefix cache hits (are they “free” or “discounted”?).
- Batch efficiency treated as either a per-request discount or as fleet-level overhead.
- Quantization changes reported without consistent deltas and guardrails.
- Overhead allocation handled differently between finance reporting, Console views, and Optimize ROI.

This ADR defines a canonical taxonomy and semantics for cost breakdown outputs used by the Standard Cost Model spec and Cost Model API.

## Decision
We will standardize on a **two-level cost breakdown taxonomy** w...

title: ADR: Audit Event Delivery Guarantees (Ingestion, Buffering, and Outage Behavior)
preview:
## Status
Published (effective for schema v1 rollout).

## Summary
This ADR defines Redwood Inference’s delivery semantics for audit events and how producers must behave during audit pipeline degradation or outages.

We choose **at-least-once delivery from the audit ingestion service onward** (durable queue → storag...
text:
## Status
Published (effective for schema v1 rollout).

## Summary
This ADR defines Redwood Inference’s delivery semantics for audit events and how producers must behave during audit pipeline degradation or outages.

We choose **at-least-once delivery from the audit ingestion service onward** (durable queue → storage/export), and **best-effort delivery from producers to ingestion** with **bounded buffering and retry**. This prioritizes platform availability (admin actions must not routinely fail) while still providing strong audit reliability, clear signals when delivery is degraded, and a consistent customer guidance story (including how to de-duplicate in SIEMs).

## Context
Redwood is standardizing audit logs across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private. Customers (especially regulated/enterprise) require:
- Consistent auditability of sensitive actions.
- Reasonable delivery latency to th...

title: Unified Change Impact Contract and Staged Migration (ADR)
preview:
Summary
-------
This ADR introduces the Unified Change Impact Contract (UCIC), a lightweight, machine- and human-readable envelope and process that standardizes how code, API, infra, model, and telemetry changes are proposed, validated, staged, and rolled back across Redwood Inference. UCIC reduces regression risk b...
text:
Summary
-------
This ADR introduces the Unified Change Impact Contract (UCIC), a lightweight, machine- and human-readable envelope and process that standardizes how code, API, infra, model, and telemetry changes are proposed, validated, staged, and rolled back across Redwood Inference. UCIC reduces regression risk by making change intent, scope, and required telemetry explicit and by codifying the staged-migration and rollback procedure across teams and runtime modes (hosted, dedicated, private).

Goals
-----
- Create a single, consistent change envelope format for service-level changes that affect: API surface, request/response schema, runtime flags, quantization/config changes, infra/topology changes, and observability surface.
- Define minimal automated checks and telemetry SLO gates that every change must satisfy prior to canary and full rollout.
- Standardize staged rollout steps...

title: ADR: SDK Timeout Model (v1) — Overall + Connect/Read Timeouts
preview:
## Status
Published (v1).

## Context
Redwood is running a cross-language SDK parity initiative (Python, TypeScript, Go) to make behavior consistent and predictable in production. A major support driver has been inconsistent timeout semantics:
- “Timeout not honored” reports (especially for streaming).
- Differences...
text:
## Status
Published (v1).

## Context
Redwood is running a cross-language SDK parity initiative (Python, TypeScript, Go) to make behavior consistent and predictable in production. A major support driver has been inconsistent timeout semantics:
- “Timeout not honored” reports (especially for streaming).
- Differences between overall deadline vs connect/read timeouts across underlying HTTP clients.
- Confusion about whether timeouts apply per attempt (when retries are enabled) or across the entire operation.

We need a single, documented timeout model and defaults that:
1) Behave similarly across languages.
2) Map cleanly onto the underlying HTTP implementations.
3) Avoid breaking changes where possible.
4) Support both non-streaming and streaming use cases.

## Decision
We will standardize on a **dual-layer timeout model** across SDKs:

1) **Overall timeout (request deadline):** `timeo...

title: ADR - Telemetry Stencil & Feature-Gate Emission Contract
preview:
Summary:
This ADR defines a compact, enforceable contract between runtime feature gates (feature flags/rollouts) and telemetry producers. We introduce the concept of a 'telemetry stencil' - a minimal, versioned emission schema that accompanies each gated feature. The stencil ensures predictable ingestion cost, bound...
text:
Summary:
This ADR defines a compact, enforceable contract between runtime feature gates (feature flags/rollouts) and telemetry producers. We introduce the concept of a 'telemetry stencil' - a minimal, versioned emission schema that accompanies each gated feature. The stencil ensures predictable ingestion cost, bounded cardinality, and privacy-safe defaults while enabling meaningful observability during staged rollouts.

Problem statement and motivation:
- Incidents in 2024-2025 exposed two repeating failure modes: runaway telemetry cardinality from experimental features and accidental emission of PII fields when new gate logic touched request structures. Both increased ingestion costs and created compliance risk.
- Teams want low-friction rollouts but need guardrails that prevent telemetry spikes, make ownership explicit, and integrate with rollout automation (canary, staged percentag...

title: Traffic Classification, Encryption, and Observability Guidelines for Runtime Services
preview:
Overview
========

text:
Overview
========

This document defines a cross-cutting standard for: (1) classifying runtime request traffic, (2) applying encryption and key-management requirements by deployment type, and (3) setting concrete observability (metrics, traces, logs) behaviors per traffic class. The goal is to ensure consistent operational posture across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private runtimes while enabling cost-aware telemetry and secure customer isolation.

Scope
-----
- Applies to all runtime services in the Redwood inference stack: api-gateway, request-router, model-runtime, kv-cache-proxy, autoscaler, and control-plane services.
- Excludes offline batch training jobs and external partner networks (those follow partner contracts).

Goals
-----
- Provide a minimal traffic taxonomy that guides routing, SLOs, and sampling.
- Mandate encryption and KMS practices appropriate to deployment sensitivity....

title: Cost Model API Guidelines (API Standards Addendum)

## Purpose
This page is an addendum to Redwood’s general API standards, defining additional requirements for the **Cost Model API**. The Cost Model API is consumed by Redwood Console and Redwood Optimize and is also used to generate finance/unit-economics primitives.

text:
## Purpose
This page is an addendum to Redwood’s general API standards, defining additional requirements for the **Cost Model API**. The Cost Model API is consumed by Redwood Console and Redwood Optimize and is also used to generate finance/unit-economics primitives.

The goals of these guidelines are to:
- Keep cost outputs **consistent and explainable** across product surfaces.
- Ensure **bounded** request/response sizes and compute cost in the presence of high-cardinality telemetry.
- Provide a stable path for **schema and semantic evolution** (versioning + deprecation).
- Define explicit expectations around **determinism**, **caching**, and **error handling**.

## Scope
These rules apply to endpoints under the Cost Model service, including (names illustrative):
- `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/estimate`
- `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/breakdown`
- `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/what...

## Status
**Accepted** (implemented behind feature gates; rollout in progress for Runtime 1.21).

## Context
Kernel auto-selection v1 relies on a small set of heuristics and a mostly global “best default” per model family. This has led to:
- Tail latency spikes (p95/p99) when long-context or mixed workloads are misc...
text:
## Status
**Accepted** (implemented behind feature gates; rollout in progress for Runtime 1.21).

## Context
Kernel auto-selection v1 relies on a small set of heuristics and a mostly global “best default” per model family. This has led to:
- Tail latency spikes (p95/p99) when long-context or mixed workloads are misclassified.
- Throughput instability across heterogeneous fleets (A100/H100/L40S) where the optimal kernel differs by SKU.
- Slow incident response when a kernel becomes unstable on a specific driver/CUDA combo; v1 lacked a consistent, centrally-managed fallback and denylist policy.

Kernel auto-selection v2 needs to:
- Improve latency and throughput stability with minimal runtime overhead.
- Preserve **correctness** and **determinism** (selection is replayable given the same inputs).
- Support safe rollout and rapid rollback with per-model and per-SKU controls.
- Be explain...

h1. Overview

This document defines the v1 API contract for *Usage Insights* in Redwood Console.

The API provides:
* Cost/usage breakdowns attributed by *route + model* (and optional dimensions: project, env, deployment mode, region).
* “Top cost drivers” and “What changed?” insights.
* CSV export job lifecycle (async generation + signed downloads).

This is an *API standards* contract. Service-specific implementation details (warehouse tables, cache strategy, dependencies) live in the system doc.

h2. Goals

* Provide stable, predictable request/response shapes for console and external tooling.
* Support cursor-based pagination and consistent filter semantics.
* Ensure time bucket boundaries are deterministic and well-defined with time zone handling.
* Provide a robust export job model (create → poll → download) with RBAC and audit logging.
* Maintain consistent totals vs billing, w...

title: Perf Canary Overhead and Safety Standards
preview:
## Summary
This document defines mandatory overhead and safety standards for the production **perf-canary** service (the always-on synthetic performance canary used to detect serving-runtime regressions per model).

These standards exist to ensure:
- Canary load remains **non-disruptive** to customer workloads (boun...
text:
## Summary
This document defines mandatory overhead and safety standards for the production **perf-canary** service (the always-on synthetic performance canary used to detect serving-runtime regressions per model).

These standards exist to ensure:
- Canary load remains **non-disruptive** to customer workloads (bounded GPU capacity and QPS).
- Canary traffic is **isolated** (least privilege, no customer data access).
- Canary telemetry is **safe-by-default** (no prompts, no PII, controlled metric cardinality).
- The canary has clear **kill switches** and rollout controls to reduce blast radius.

## Scope
Applies to:
- The **perf-canary** service and any sidecars/jobs that generate synthetic inference traffic in production.
- Related telemetry (metrics, traces, logs) produced by canary runs.
- Control-plane configuration surfaces that enable/disable canary per region/tier/model.

Does ...

title: ADR-0127: SLO breach evaluation strategy for rollout auto-actions (burn-rate, sample gating, debounce/holdoff)
preview:
## Status
Published (effective for Auto-rollback on SLO breach MVP)

## Context
Redwood’s rollout controller (canary, staged rollouts, and A/B) is being integrated with latency and error-rate SLO monitors to automatically **pause** or **rollback** a rollout when an SLO breach occurs.

A naïve “threshold exceeded -> ...
text:
## Status
Published (effective for Auto-rollback on SLO breach MVP)

## Context
Redwood’s rollout controller (canary, staged rollouts, and A/B) is being integrated with latency and error-rate SLO monitors to automatically **pause** or **rollback** a rollout when an SLO breach occurs.

A naïve “threshold exceeded -> rollback immediately” implementation causes **oscillation** and **false positives** due to:
- Natural tail latency variability during small-traffic canary steps.
- Metric ingestion lag and partial telemetry outages.
- Transient bursts (GC pauses, noisy neighbors, autoscaler step changes).
- SLO monitor windowing effects (brief spikes shouldn’t always cause action).

We need a breach evaluation strategy that is:
- Sensitive to real regressions (fast detection).
- Robust against noise (avoids flapping/paging storms).
- Consistent across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployme...

text:
Overview

Purpose
This playbook defines our standard approach for fusing telemetry signals into meaningful alerts and for controlling alert noise across Redwood Inference services. It provides architecture guidance, scoring formulas, tuning procedures, and rollout steps so teams can deliver high-signal, low-noise operational alerts that map to customer impact and SLO risk.

Scope
- Applies to all production services (hosted API, Dedicated, Private) and platform components that emit SLO-relevant metrics and traces.
- Covers alert creation, signal fusion, deduplication, severity scoring, suppression, and on-call routing.
- Does not replace service-specific runbooks; teams should reference this playbook and add service-level overrides.

Definitions
- Primary signal: A direct SLO or user-facing metric (p95 latency, error-rate, request success).
- Secondary signal: Supporting telemetry (CP...

title: Contract Survivability and Degradation Test Plan
preview:
Summary
This page defines the Contract Survivability and Degradation Test Plan: a standardized, automated validation and exercise suite for verifying that service contracts (API request/response schemas, streaming semantics, model-variant compatibility) survive realistic failure and degradation modes, and that grace...
text:
Summary
This page defines the Contract Survivability and Degradation Test Plan: a standardized, automated validation and exercise suite for verifying that service contracts (API request/response schemas, streaming semantics, model-variant compatibility) survive realistic failure and degradation modes, and that graceful-degradation policies keep downstream applications safe.

Purpose and scope
- Purpose: Ensure changes to APIs, model variants, runtime kernels, or infra do not cause silent client breakage or unacceptable user-facing quality regressions when the platform degrades (capacity loss, quantization fallbacks, network partitions, high tail latency).
- Scope: Applies to: Redwood public REST/streaming API endpoints, model-serving runtime variants (fp16, int8, quantized kernels), routing/fallback policies, and platform-side contract transforms (stream chunking, partial result check...

title: ADR-0128: Rollout audit trail schema for SLO-triggered auto actions (pause/rollback/suppress)
preview:
## Status
Published.

## Decision drivers
Redwood is adding automated mitigation during model rollouts (canary/A/B/staged) based on latency and error-rate SLO monitors. When the rollout controller pauses, rolls back, or suppresses an action, we need an audit trail that is:

- **Forensic-grade**: sufficient context t...
text:
## Status
Published.

## Decision drivers
Redwood is adding automated mitigation during model rollouts (canary/A/B/staged) based on latency and error-rate SLO monitors. When the rollout controller pauses, rolls back, or suppresses an action, we need an audit trail that is:

- **Forensic-grade**: sufficient context to answer “what happened?”, “why?”, and “what did we do?”
- **Consistent across environments**: Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments should emit the same shape
- **Queryable and linkable**: operators should be able to jump from an audit event to the rollout, monitor definition, and metrics snapshot
- **Safe**: avoid storing customer prompts/outputs or other sensitive payloads; keep cardinality bounded
- **Composable**: supports additional signals later (e.g., eval-based regressions) without breaking existing consumers

## Context
We already emit operational logs and me...

title: ADR: SDK Retry Policy v1 (Defaults, Configurability, and Streaming Guidance)
preview:
## Summary
This ADR defines the v1 retry policy for Redwood Inference SDKs (Python, TypeScript, Go). The goal is to make retry behavior consistent, predictable, and safe across languages while protecting the platform from retry storms.

Key outcomes:
- Standardized retryable conditions (HTTP status codes + network f...
text:
## Summary
This ADR defines the v1 retry policy for Redwood Inference SDKs (Python, TypeScript, Go). The goal is to make retry behavior consistent, predictable, and safe across languages while protecting the platform from retry storms.

Key outcomes:
- Standardized retryable conditions (HTTP status codes + network failures).
- Standardized exponential backoff with jitter and `Retry-After` handling.
- Standardized default maximum attempts and a small set of configuration knobs.
- Conservative streaming retry behavior with explicit idempotency guidance.

## Status
**Published** (v1). SDKs should implement this policy by default in the next coordinated major/minor releases per the parity rollout plan.

## Context / Problem
Customers have reported cross-language inconsistencies such as:
- Different retryable status code sets (including incorrect retries on 4xx).
- Ignoring `Retry-After` o...

title: KV Cache Fragmentation + Compaction Metrics Standard

- KV compaction activity and overhead
- KV eviction (tier-aware LRU and related reasons)

The goal is consistent, low-cardinality telemetry that allows us to:
1) d...
text:
## Purpose
This document standardizes the metric names, definitions, label schema, and required dashboards/alerts for:
- KV cache allocator fragmentation
- KV compaction activity and overhead
- KV eviction (tier-aware LRU and related reasons)

The goal is consistent, low-cardinality telemetry that allows us to:
1) detect fragmentation before it causes tail latency spikes or OOM
2) quantify compaction overhead and validate compaction budgeting
3) monitor eviction correctness/safety and customer impact
4) correlate allocator health with decode p95/p99 and request shedding

## Scope
Applies to the serving runtime KV cache allocator and scheduler-integrated compaction/eviction.

Out of scope:
- Customer UI surfacing in Console (tracked separately)
- Generic GPU memory metrics unrelated to KV arena (use platform standards)

## Definitions
### KV arena
The runtime-managed memory pool used f...

title: Config Consistency and Gated Feature Flags Playbook

text:
Overview:
This playbook defines our standard architecture and operational practices for distributing runtime configuration (feature flags, quantization/precision profiles, autoscaling hints, and routing policies) with a focus on strong eventual consistency, safe progressive rollouts, and automated SLI/SLO safety gates. It is intended for platform engineers, runtime teams, and service owners who operate multi-tenant inference services (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).

Problem statement:
- Runtime configuration changes (flags, model variants, quantization profiles) are frequent and can change behavior across thousands of nodes instantly.
- Inconsistencies between control-plane config and node application config cause correctness, performance regressions, and hard-to-debug incidents.
- Feature-flag-driven rollouts (canary, ring, percentage rollouts) must be reversible with bounded blast rad...

Overview

Purpose:
This playbook captures Redwood's recommended patterns for minimizing API/service surface area and evolving request/response schemas with minimal consumer disruption. It consolidates practical rules, examples, testing strategies, and rollout procedures so teams can safely ship changes that maintain production stability and predictable client cost/latency profiles.

Scope:
Applies to all externally-facing Redwood APIs (hosted and private control plane) and internal service-to-service contracts that impact inference, telemetry, billing, or routing decisions. It complements the API compatibility & deprecation policy but focuses on design patterns and operational controls.

Definitions:
- Service surface: set of public endpoints, RPC methods, query parameters, header hooks, and documented request/response fields a consumer depends on.

title: ADR-0142: Perf Canary Baselines, Regression Thresholds, Min Sample Sizes, and Anti-Flap Logic
preview:

Published.

This ADR defines how the production performance canary computes baselines and evaluates regressions for per-model serving performance (primarily p95 latency and tokens/sec). It documents:
- Baseline computation approach (rolling vs fixed) and when each is used.
- Regression threshold...
text:
## Status
Published.

## Summary
This ADR defines how the production performance canary computes baselines and evaluates regressions for per-model serving performance (primarily p95 latency and tokens/sec). It documents:
- Baseline computation approach (rolling vs fixed) and when each is used.
- Regression thresholds (warning vs paging) and how they are applied.
- Minimum sample sizes and confidence requirements to avoid noisy alerts.
- Anti-flap / dedupe logic to prevent alert storms during drift, telemetry delays, or small-sample variance.

## Context / Problem
Redwood’s serving runtime changes frequently (kernel selection updates, scheduler heuristics, model config changes, GPU fleet changes, and staged rollouts). We need an always-on canary that:
- Detects real p95 latency regressions and throughput drops early.
- Produces actionable signals for attribution (kernel vs scheduler vs...

title: SLI Specification: API Gateway + Smart Routing (Tiered SLO Program)

This specification is used to:
- Compute tiered SLOs and error budgets for **Hosted (shared)** and **Ded...
text:
## Purpose
This document defines the canonical measurement standard (SLIs) for Redwood’s customer-facing inference request path across the **API Gateway** and **Smart Routing / Control Plane policy evaluation**.

This specification is used to:
- Compute tiered SLOs and error budgets for **Hosted (shared)** and **Dedicated (reserved capacity)**.
- Ensure consistent accounting across regions, endpoints, retries, routing fallback, and streaming.
- Prevent metric/cardinality explosions while preserving debuggability.

**Non-goals:** This document does not define the SLO targets themselves. Refer to the tiered SLO definition page for targets and reporting windows.

## Scope
### In-scope components
- API Gateway (public edge): authentication, request validation, rate limiting, request forwarding, streaming relay.
- Routing / Control Plane policy evaluation: tier-aware routing decision, regi...

title: ADR-046: KV cache eviction policy (tier-aware LRU with prefix-cache coordination)
preview:
## Status
Published (runtime-1.22)

## Summary
We will implement **tier-aware LRU eviction at the “sequence/session” granularity**, coordinated with the **prefix/KV reuse cache**, and integrated with scheduler/backpressure. The policy is designed to:
- reduce burst-driven OOM / forced shedding caused by KV fragmenta...
text:
## Status
Published (runtime-1.22)

## Summary
We will implement **tier-aware LRU eviction at the “sequence/session” granularity**, coordinated with the **prefix/KV reuse cache**, and integrated with scheduler/backpressure. The policy is designed to:
- reduce burst-driven OOM / forced shedding caused by KV fragmentation and long-lived allocations
- avoid correctness hazards (no eviction of in-flight decode state)
- preserve customer-visible quality and latency for higher tiers

In short: **evict safe, low-priority, least-recently-used sequences first; protect pinned/high-tier/in-flight work; prefer dropping reusable prefix cache before killing active sessions when possible.**

## Context and problem statement
The KV compaction project introduces mechanisms to reduce GPU memory fragmentation and smooth p95/p99 latency. However, compaction alone cannot guarantee bounded memory usage und...

title: ADR-0247: Route normalization and cost attribution semantics for Usage Insights
preview:
## Summary

The decision introduces:
- A shared **route normalization** library used at ingestion and query time.
- A can...
text:
## Summary
This ADR defines how Redwood canonicalizes API routes for reporting and how we attribute usage and cost across retries, fallbacks, and model aliases for the **In-console usage insights** feature.

The decision introduces:
- A shared **route normalization** library used at ingestion and query time.
- A canonical **route key** and **route group** for stable aggregation (guarding against cardinality blow-ups).
- A **model bucketing** scheme (family + serving profile + version bucket) that resolves aliases.
- Explicit attribution rules for **retries** and **fallbacks** so totals reconcile with billing and are explainable to users.

## Status
Published. Effective for Usage Insights Beta, with compatibility shims for existing metering events.

## Context / Problem
Usage Insights needs to break down cost by *route* and *model* (e.g., `/v1/chat/completions` on `llama-3.1-70b`). Tod...

title: Interactive batch multiplexing and client pacing specification
preview:
Summary
-------
This specification defines Redwood's standard for interactive batch multiplexing and client pacing to improve GPU efficiency while meeting per-tenant latency SLOs. It covers the client↔edge pacing handshake, server admission and multiplexing algorithms, QoS profiles, telemetry requirements, and safe ...
text:
Summary
-------
This specification defines Redwood's standard for interactive batch multiplexing and client pacing to improve GPU efficiency while meeting per-tenant latency SLOs. It covers the client↔edge pacing handshake, server admission and multiplexing algorithms, QoS profiles, telemetry requirements, and safe rollout steps. The goal is measurable throughput improvements for bursty, low-token interactive workloads with bounded impact to p99/p50 latency SLOs.

Context and motivation
----------------------
Many Redwood customers issue many small, low-token inference calls (e.g., chat type interactions). Serving these as independent requests leaves GPUs underutilized and increases per-token cost. Server-side batching helps but introduces head-of-line latency and unpredictable tail behavior when workloads are bursty.

Interactive batch multiplexing pairs a lightweight client pacing c...

title: ADR: Shadow Traffic Telemetry Data Model + Cardinality Guardrails (v1)
preview:
## Status
**Accepted** (v1 for Beta + GA). Any changes to required fields or dimension allowlists require Observability + SRE review.

## Context
Shadow traffic duplicates a sampled subset of production requests to one or more shadow variants (model/version/quantization/prompt/routing policy) and evaluates outputs a...
text:
## Status
**Accepted** (v1 for Beta + GA). Any changes to required fields or dimension allowlists require Observability + SRE review.

## Context
Shadow traffic duplicates a sampled subset of production requests to one or more shadow variants (model/version/quantization/prompt/routing policy) and evaluates outputs asynchronously. We need:
- Reliable pairing of control + shadow responses for quality deltas.
- Enough dimensions to power Console drilldowns and regression alerts.
- Strict controls to prevent high-cardinality blowups in metrics, logs, and analytics tables.

This ADR defines the event schemas emitted by the data plane and scoring pipeline, the required dimensions (tags/labels) permitted for aggregation, and enforcement rules.

## Decision
We will emit **two canonical telemetry event types** for shadow traffic in v1:
1) `shadow.pair.v1` — a single “pair summary” event that r...

title: Quality Gates Observability Standard (Optimize)
preview:
## Purpose
This standard defines the required metrics, logs, and traces for **Quality Gate** runs used to approve or block Optimize configuration changes (batching policy, prefix/KV caching settings, quantization profile switches). The goal is to make gate decisions **auditable, attributable, debuggable**, and opera...
text:
## Purpose
This standard defines the required metrics, logs, and traces for **Quality Gate** runs used to approve or block Optimize configuration changes (batching policy, prefix/KV caching settings, quantization profile switches). The goal is to make gate decisions **auditable, attributable, debuggable**, and operationally safe.

This page is normative for:
- Optimize service (recommendations + apply boundary enforcement)
- Eval-harness (gate execution)
- Rollout controller (promotion blocking)
- Console API (display of gate status and artifact links)

## Definitions
- **Gate run**: An evaluation job comparing *candidate config* vs *baseline config* for a specific policy target (route / model / account).
- **Gate tier**:
 - **smoke** (fast): bounded runtime/cost; intended for rapid feedback
 - **full**: broader prompt coverage and stricter statistical checks
- **Gate decision state**...

title: Streaming Error Taxonomy and Retry Contract
preview:
Overview:
This page defines a company-wide error taxonomy and client-facing retry contract specifically for streaming endpoints (server-sent events, HTTP/2/gRPC streaming, and chunked streaming). It supplements existing API compatibility and typed-error guidelines by focusing on streaming-specific failure modes, res...
text:
Overview:
This page defines a company-wide error taxonomy and client-facing retry contract specifically for streaming endpoints (server-sent events, HTTP/2/gRPC streaming, and chunked streaming). It supplements existing API compatibility and typed-error guidelines by focusing on streaming-specific failure modes, resume semantics, and SLO-safe retry behavior. The goal is to make client behaviour predictable, reduce unnecessary load from aggressive retries, and provide observability hooks that map streaming errors to operational responses and SLIs.

Audience:
- API owners and backend engineers implementing streaming endpoints
- Client SDK authors and integrators
- SRE and monitoring owners responsible for SLIs/alerting

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to all external-facing streaming endpoints and internal long-lived RPC streams used for inference (chat, generation streams, long-poll...

title: ADR-0142: Tier-aware SLI tagging at ingress (Hosted vs Dedicated)
preview:
## Status
Published (effective for SLO reporting + error budget enforcement starting Q1 2026 rollout).

## Context / Problem
Redwood is formalizing tiered SLOs and error budgets for **Hosted (shared)** vs **Dedicated (reserved capacity)**. We must be able to compute SLIs (success rate, latency percentiles, streaming...
text:
## Status
Published (effective for SLO reporting + error budget enforcement starting Q1 2026 rollout).

## Context / Problem
Redwood is formalizing tiered SLOs and error budgets for **Hosted (shared)** vs **Dedicated (reserved capacity)**. We must be able to compute SLIs (success rate, latency percentiles, streaming reliability) with correct tier attribution across:
- API Gateway handling (auth, rate limits, request normalization)
- Smart Routing (policy evaluation, fallback, retries)
- Model serving request path (batching, execution)
- Observability pipelines (metrics, logs, traces)

We have recurring reliability escalations tied to burst traffic where:
- Requests may be **retried** or **routed to fallback capacity**.
- Failures can occur at multiple layers (gateway, routing, serving, stream transport).
- Dedicated customers need **account-level visibility**, while Hosted must avoid ...

title: ADR-0231: Require quality evaluation gates for Optimize config changes
preview:
## Status
**Accepted** (effective for Hosted API Optimize applies starting limited production rollout).

## Decision
Redwood Inference will require **quality evaluation gates** to pass **before** any Optimize recommendation (or manual Optimize-driven change) can be applied to **production traffic** for the following...
text:
## Status
**Accepted** (effective for Hosted API Optimize applies starting limited production rollout).

## Decision
Redwood Inference will require **quality evaluation gates** to pass **before** any Optimize recommendation (or manual Optimize-driven change) can be applied to **production traffic** for the following config change types:

- **Batching policy updates** (e.g., max batch size, continuous batching parameters)
- **Prefix/KV caching settings** (e.g., cache enablement, eviction policies, cache keying changes)
- **Quantization profile changes** (e.g., fp16 -> int8, model-family-specific quant profiles per GPU SKU)

Gate enforcement is **mandatory** for:
- One-click “Apply” in Console
- Any Optimize auto-apply mechanism
- Canary promotion in rollout controller (defense-in-depth)

A change may only proceed when:
1) **Static validation** passes (unsupported combinations rejected ...
